comment_content:
This is a broad UG-level course to HCI, so the overall content is quite general. Mostly the example of the technology is not super fancy one, but you can learn more through the application. A brief outline is right here.
1. Human-Centric Design
2. Understanding Humans
3. Design Thinking Process (Empathize-Ideate-Define-Prototype-Testing)
4. Multimodal Interaction
5. Ubiquitous Computing
6. Evaluation Process
7. Extended Reality
8. CSCW and social computing
9. Crowd Computing
10. Computing for Good (Persuasion & Accessibility)
The most special part is that it also mentions some psychology of humans and some design principles. And no code is mentioned in the lecture, so it is comfortable to listen the lectures. Overall it is worth for designers and programmers to take. (I know some programmers may need some times to adopt the design thinking mindset, not solution-driven but problem-driven. It may be “time-consuming” but it is a great chance to cooperate with other programmers and designers.)
There are 3 lab sessions to assist you for the projects (so you only need to attend those labs). TAs will show how you can build your prototype (this year they teach AI voice chatbot and unity). Of course you can use your own way to make the prototype, as long as you can show the effect you want.
The course outline and project descriptions is uploaded as a reference.
comment_teaching: Prof Ma is quite nice. She will ask questions during the lecture, and reply emails very fast. If you have any questions, she also feel free to answer. The teaching team is also helpful.
comment_workload:
As you see from other comments, there are many assignments and small tasks in this course.

There will be some (around 4-5) pop-up in-class exercise at the end of the lecture, and you need to submit by the end of that day or next day after the lecture. As long as you submit your work and finish it by following the lectures notes, you can get your participation marks. It is a good chance for review but it is quite messy. (The most tricky part is that prof wont upload the whole instruction to canvas. If you skip the lecture then you will have no idea about the exercise, Better to have a friend with you)

For the projects, there are many components. I will try my best to list out. Except Project1, we need to have a presentation with a demo video (around 8 minutes), no need live demo so relax. During the presentations, you have to give marks to other groups as peer evaluation. (Yes, your marks are from teaching team and other classmates. ) And after the presentation, you have to update your own portfolio page by putting your reflection and contribution. (You can build the page whatever you can, like Wix studio, Google Sites, guihub, Framer etc)

And for the final video paper, it is pretty chill. You just show your idea for the given topic and present it through a video. No need to be so practical (no need to make a demo), just need to be fun and creative.

Overall I think the workload is large but manageable. Don't be deadline fighter, and don't think you can finish the project within a few days. Start earlier must be helpful at all. This course also helps you to build a portfolio page, and you can learn from other students (There are many exchange students in the class!). It is still worth to take, no matter you are programmer or designer.
comment_grading:
This course is using experimental learning.
Project1 - 5%
Project2 - 20%
Project3 - 20%
2 in-class midterms - 35%
Final Video Paper - 10%
Participation - 10%

You can pick your own groupmates. Prof have asked if we want same teammates for all 3 projects or change teams for each projects (we picked the first one). The group size depends on the class size (this year we have 5 ppl per group). And the teaching team doesn’t give a clear rubric for the projects.

Both midterms are open-notes. No need to write codes. The weighting of the 2 midterms are slightly different. Midterm1 only allows paper notes and midterm2 you can bring a laptop to watch a video regarding to the question.

For your reference:
Project1 - 4.5/5
Project2 - 17.84/20
Project3 - 17.15/20
Midterm1 - 73/100
Midterm2 - 81/100 (Mean: 81.8; SD: 13.4842)
Final Video Paper - 72.5/100 (Mean: 67.3; SD: 8.69)
Participation - 7.5/10
Total: 81.2 (Letter grade: A-)

I think the course is quite competitive, as everyone get very high marks in all assignments. If you want a better score, you may need study hard in midterms.

comment_content:
Content is classic computer science meets humanities. Design paradigms, a method for how to develop an app starting with getting user needs.....interviewing (though you don't actually have to interview) brainstorming, mindmapping, prototyping etc etc.

Lectures with 1000s of pictures and video links to cool projects (some outdated from 2010s). But the content is not like a core subject where you need to strain yourself a lot to remember it. If you go to class and engage with it, you can find it interesting.

In reality you end up memorizing a few key terms for design (Gestalt principles etc) and the whole step by step need finding to product process. To get a good grade on the exams, you need to write fast and alot, but its not that hard. (its mostly common sense stuff e.g point out the design flaws of the mcdonalds kisok using principles from class, draw out a new UI for this website highlight the principles you used)
comment_teaching: Teacher is passionate and kind, she goes through the slides and asks simple questions for a discussion. When you get to an actual tangible theory part e.g a definition or a pros and cons, best to note it down as that is testable.
comment_workload:
A lot of work every week or 2 weeks if you want an A, there is constantly a diary entry, an exercise or a group work to do but they are not very hard just a bit long.

If you get a lazy group you will suffer (or if you don't care you can all coast and get a B- or something). Just do the slides early and think of an interesting idea.
Compress the notes down to a few pages per lecture for the exams.
comment_grading:
Open book midterm 1) You have 1h20 so you need to write as fast as possible, honestly anything as long as it makes sense will get marks.
Open notes with laptop midterm 2)1h20) Your Laptop is used to watch a video which there is a question on, and to store notes to use. The rest is like midterm 1 a bunch or writing/designing questions that you need to write quickly/ scan your notes for a definition or example answer.

Randomly,  there will be an exercise  to do related to the class due next day. That is only known to people who went to class (unless you have a friend to tell you). This is the 'participation grade' 10% there where 4 tasks for this sem.

Group projects are peer reviewed so its a bit meh, you end up getting around 14-18 /20% depending on how much effort your group put in.
Video project (solo) is 10%, and peer reviewed.

My grades: (High effort)
Project 1 (5%), 4.5
Project 2 (20%),17.78
Project 3 (20%), 17.73
Midterm Exam 1 (20.1% adjusted weight), 74
Midterm Exam 2 (14.9% adjusted weight),93
Total Midterm (35%),29.225
Final Video Paper (10%), 7.754
Participation (10%) 9

Overall 85.99/100
Letter: Not announced yet\

NO FINAL EXAM IS A BIG BONUS

comment_content:
2019 Fall Projects
- project 1: interactive ad
- project 2: VR/AR
- project 3: robot (program Pepper/Nao robot to be used for educational purpose)
- video paper: collective well-being

This course is super hands-on. You get a chance to try out different much-hyped techs. This is great in two ways: you get to find out whether you are interested in these fields, and you can have a rough idea of the power and limitation of these techs.

This course is also a good chance to build your portfolio.
comment_teaching:
Killer Features
- Design Process Drill: for each project, we went through the process of selecting user group, identifying problems, brainstorming solutions, creating storyboards, speed-dating with users, prototyping, getting user feedback, etc. Gradually helps me build up a good habit.
- Teamwork Training: each project is a chance to practice and improve my teamwork skills. How do we come to an agreement? How do we divide up work? How do we keep track of progress? These soft skills, unlike hard technical knowledge, will be valuable no matter what job you do in the future.

Also
- teacher: friendly, interactive
- lecture: lots of videos and pictures, no equations
- classmates: stacked; and lots of international students
comment_workload:
- high workload (many hours)
- they are enjoyable hours though (if you enjoy project more than assignments and exams)
- better take it when your other courses are not heavy
comment_grading:
Grading is fair.
Not easy grade, but fair.
Reflects quality of my projects.

comment_content:
The content is interesting and covers all the basics of HCI. It provides a solid overview and explains things like prototyping and user testing as well as more advanced concepts later on. It covers a lot of ground, but is not really able to go in depth.
The projects allow students to put everything into practice.
comment_teaching: The lectures are nice. Not particularly exciting, but usually interesting and relevant.
comment_workload: The group projects require a lot of teamwork and efficient planning is key. It is very easy to spend many hours on small things that will hardly impact your final presentation, since you have very limited time to present and the grade for the project only relies on the presentation.
comment_grading:
Feedback and grading of assignments and presentation is very unclear. Since it depends mostly on peer evaluation of a short presentation, oftentimes it is not so much about the work that was actually put into the project but simply whether the presentation was funny. And while it is of course important to present results well, I think it is sometimes hard to put a  lot of work into user testing and prototyping only to briefly glance over all that in favor of making a few jokes to get good peer grades.
Essentially, to get a good grade the presentation needs to be fun and interesting, while the content is somewhat secondary.

comment_content:
The content mainly focuses on how you can take a humanistic approach while designing tech products.

There is a lot of idea generation, brainstorming, market research and the prototyping.

There is a lot of interesting course content, but it is not very applicable to the projects that we do in class, good for future reference though if you are interested in this field.
comment_teaching:
Professor Ma is very experienced in HCI and has put in a lot of effort to design this course. The project prompts are always interesting.

Classes are fun with a lot of videos and image-based content, sometimes there are activities like design thinking, brainstorming etc.

My only contention would be that professor does not give feedback on the projects. A lot of students worked very hard on their projects, and they never get any feedback, only ambiguous scores from the audience (other students), which is never reflective of whether your idea was actually good or not.
comment_workload: Quite doable if you have a good team.
comment_grading:
Three group projects 60% –
Project 1 15% – Redesign a restaurant ordering app
Project 2 20% – Mixed Reality
Project 3 25% - Human-Robot Interaction for typhoon situation

Midterm: 15% - Open notes, quite easy and interesting
Video Paper: 15% - Create a video on a product that gives happiness  
Participation + Bonus: 10% - self explanatory

The whole course is peer evaluated, so make sure whatever you do, it appeals to the audience
